elements.sent-2411-001: Quantum networks aim to communicate distant quantum devices, such as quantum computers.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this context, a critical requirement is the secure and reliable transmission of arbitrary quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-003: Quantum teleportation is widely used to transmit arbitrary quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, it requires entanglement swapping and purification to distribute entanglements over long distances, introducing significant overhead and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-005: These challenges limit its practicality for real-world quantum communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address this limitation, we propose a novel scheme for directly transmitting quantum states encoded using error-correction codes.
elements.sent-2411-007: The proposed scheme leverages the robustness of quantum error correction codes to ensure secure and reliable quantum communication.
elements.sent-2411-008: By encoding quantum states with error-correction codes and strategically injecting uncorrectable errors, we enhance the security and reliability of the transmission process.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our approach reduces the overhead associated with entanglement distribution and provides a high tolerance for transmission errors.
elements.sent-2411-010: This study presents an advancement in practical and scalable quantum communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-011: A prominent feature is the inherent security of various quantum technologies.
elements.sent-2411-012: To achieve this, it is essential to efficiently and securely transmit arbitrary quantum states over long distances.
elements.sent-2411-013: Quantum teleportation is a quantum protocol that utilizes quantum entanglement states, as well as quantum and classical links, to transmit arbitrary quantum states~\cite{qt00, qt01, qt02 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, quantum teleportation requires a pre-shared entangled pair between the sender and receiver.
elements.sent-2411-015: If the distance is long, entanglement swapping is required to extend the range~\cite{es00, es01, es02 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In addition, to enhance the fidelity of shared entanglement, entanglement purification~\cite{ep00, ep01, ep02, ep03, ep04, ep05, ep06 must be performed, as reported in~\cite{lded00 .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.7\linewidth]{LDED.eps}} \caption{Schematic of the long-distance entanglement distribution Blue spheres represent the entangled pairs and gray spheres represent the ancilla qubits used for purification. By repeatedly performing entanglement purification to enhance fidelity and entanglement swapping, entangled pairs can be shared over long distances.} \label{fig:lded}
elements.sent-2411-017: Entanglement swapping has a success probability of 50\% for physical Bell state measurement (BSM) in a linear optical setup, causing the success probability to decrease exponentially with the number of nodes over long distances.
elements.sent-2411-018: To mitigate this, logical BSM using quantum error correction codes (QECCs) can be performed, increasing the success probability to $1-1/2^{n_{bsm }$ based on the code length $n_{bsm}$, this introduces an $n_{bsm}$-fold overhead~\cite{lbsm00, lbsm01 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: When purification is considered, the overhead increases further.
elements.sent-2411-020: Using the purification method described in~\cite{ep06 , high fidelity can be achieved with only two ancillary qubits per entangled pair (e.g., approximately 0.995 fidelity with a physical error rate of 0.1).}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, if the distance between the sender and receiver is significant and requires multiple stages of entanglement swapping and purification, as illustrated in figure~\ref{fig:lded .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In that case, the overhead can increase exponentially with the number of relay nodes.
elements.sent-2411-023: Given these challenges, if overhead is inevitable and qubit transmission is necessary for entanglement distribution, it may be efficient to encode the information to be sent using QECCs and transmit it in a manner similar to classical communication~\cite{qec00, qec01, qec02, qec03, qec04, qec05, qec06, qec07, qec08 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Nevertheless, quantum teleportation, which can transmit arbitrary quantum states using only entangled pairs and classical bits, inherently possesses a degree of security because entangled pairs do not contain any information about the transmitted quantum states~\cite{qt02 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus, for a fair comparison, security should be ensured when transmitting encoded quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this study, we introduced a novel scheme that encodes quantum states with QECCs and injects uncorrectable errors to enable secure and reliable long-distance transmission of arbitrary quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our approach addresses the limitations of current quantum state transmission methods and provides a scalable solution for quantum communication.
elements.section-2411-002: Uncorrectable-error-injection-based reliable and secure quantum communication
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.95\linewidth]{pro11.eps} \caption{Schematic of the proposed scheme: (a) The sender prepares the arbitrary quantum states to be transmitted. Then, the sender randomly intersperses the dummy states $|D\rangle$ defined in MUBs with the quantum state intended for transmission. (b) The sender encodes them and injects the uncorrectable error $E_{un}$. (c) The sender transmits the error syndrome $s$ and $|\psi\rangle_{T}$. Then, the receiver performs error correction based on the syndrome $s$ (d) The receiver sends the $ACK_1$ to the sender upon receiving the quantum state. (e) The sender informs the receiver of $E_{un}$. The receiver applies it to the received state and performs syndrome extraction to verify if a zero vector is obtained. If the syndrome is not a zero vector, the sender and receiver abort the process. (f) The receiver sends $ACK_2$ to the sender upon confirming the all-zero syndrome. (g) Upon receiving $ACK_2$, the sender transmits $P_{\kappa^4}$ and $U_{MUB}$ to the receiver. (h) The receiver uses the received $P_{\kappa^4}$ and $U_{MUB}$ to verify the errors in $|D\rangle$. If no errors are detected, the process continues; otherwise, the data is aborted.} \label{fig:pro}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this section, we describe a scheme for transmitting quantum states in a reliable and secure manner by injecting uncorrectable errors into the encoded states.
elements.sent-2411-029: We consider a system model in which a noisy and insecure quantum channel and an authenticated classical channel are connected between the sender and receiver.
elements.sent-2411-030: The classical channel must be authenticated because the proposed scheme must prevent man-in-the-middle attacks, such as spoofing~\cite{sa00 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, we consider scenarios in which arbitrary quantum states, such as the intermediate results of quantum computing, are sent once, rather than repeatedly sending the same quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-032: To transmit arbitrary quantum states, the sender first measures the quantum bit error rate of the quantum channel.
elements.sent-2411-033: Based on this information, the sender determines the error correction capability $t$ of the QECCs and selects an $[[n, k, d]]$ QECC.
elements.sent-2411-034: To facilitate the security assessment discussed in the APPENDIX, we consider only non-degenerate quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-035: A non-degenerate code satisfies the condition that each correctable error yields linearly independent results when applied to elements of the code~\cite{qec04, qec07 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Subsequently, the sender generates four bit-strings to create four encryption keys, $\mathbf{\kappa^1}$, $\mathbf{\kappa^2}$, $\mathbf{\kappa^3}$, and $\mathbf{\kappa^4}$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Among these, $\mathbf{\kappa^1}$, $\mathbf{\kappa^2}$, and $\mathbf{\kappa^3}$ have a length of $k-k'$ bits, $\mathbf{\kappa^4}$ has a length of $k$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-038: The quantum state that the sender wishes to transmit is an arbitrary $k'$-qubit information $|\psi' \rangle = \sum_{i ^{2^{k' c_i |m_i \rangle$ where $|m_i\rangle$ denotes the computational basis states and $c_i$ are the complex amplitudes associated with the basis states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: To prevent an eavesdropper from intercepting the entire quantum state and then transmitting spoofed data pretending to be the sender, that is, intercept-and-resend attacks, or from extracting information using ancilla states, all possible unitary transformations, and measurements, we randomly mix $k-k'$ dummy states $|D\rangle$ of two sets of states, $\{|0\rangle, |1\rangle \}$ and $\{|+\rangle, |-\rangle \}$, from mutually unbiased bases (MUBs) at random positions.
elements.sent-2411-040: This can be expressed as \begin{equation}|D\rangle = \bigotimes_{i=1}^{k-k'} X^{\mathbf{\kappa^{1}_{i}}}Z^{\mathbf{\kappa^{2}_{i}}}H^{\mathbf{\kappa^{3}_{i}}} |0\rangle = U_{MUB}|0\rangle^{\otimes k-k'},\end{equation} where $X$ and $Z$ are Pauli operators, $H$ is the Hadamard operator, and $X^{\mathbf{\kappa^{1 _{i }$, $Z^{\mathbf{\kappa^{2 _{i }$, and $H^{\mathbf{\kappa^{3 _{i }$ denote operators that apply the respective operator when the bit is 1, and apply the identity operator when the bit is 0.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Additionally, the $U_{MUB}$ is an operator that encodes zero ancillary states into mutually unbiased states.
elements.sent-2411-042: The prepared states must be encoded using QECCs to transmit quantum state reliably.
elements.sent-2411-043: Thus, the $n$-qubit state $|\psi \rangle$ prepared by the sender for $[[n, k, d]]$ QECC encoding can be expressed as \begin{equation}|\psi \rangle =(P_{\kappa^4}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k})|\psi' \rangle U_{MUB}|0\rangle^{\otimes k-k'} |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k} \nonumber = (P_{\kappa^4}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k})(I^{\otimes k'}\otimes U_{MUB}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k}) |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'} \nonumber =U_c |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'}\end{equation} where $P_{\kappa^4}$ is a permutation operator that permutes a qubit of $|D\rangle$ where ones are located in $\kappa^4$.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}|\psi \rangle =(P_{\kappa^4}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k})|\psi' \rangle U_{MUB}|0\rangle^{\otimes k-k'} |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k} \nonumber = (P_{\kappa^4}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k})(I^{\otimes k'}\otimes U_{MUB}\otimes I^{\otimes n-k}) |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'} \nonumber =U_c |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: The sender encodes $|\psi \rangle$ into a logical state using the encoding operator $U_E$ of the selected QECC \begin{equation}|\psi\rangle_{L} = U_E|\psi\rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: To perform encryption, a random Pauli error operator $E_{un}$, which the chosen QECC cannot correct, is injected into the encoded logical state $|\psi \rangle_L$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The uncorrectable error $E_{un}$ is injected into the transmitted state, preventing eavesdroppers or intermediate nodes from obtaining information about the state.
elements.sent-2411-047: Additionally, after receiving the state, the receiver uses it to verify whether the received state is indeed the one sent by the sender, functioning somewhat like a signature.
elements.sent-2411-048: These functionalities will be elaborated in section~\ref{dd and the set of $E_{un}$ that the sender can choose is detailed in the APPENDIX.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The resulting encoded and encrypted state $|\psi \rangle_E$ is as follows: \begin{equation}|\psi \rangle_E = E_{un}|\psi\rangle_{L}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: The sender calculates the syndrome $s$ of the injected $E_{un}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The sender transmits the encoded state $|\psi \rangle_E$ through the quantum channel and sends the syndrome $s$ through the classical channel to the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-052: Upon receiving $|\psi \rangle_E$ and $s$, the receiver extracts the syndrome of $|\psi \rangle_E$ and performs error correction based on $s$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Generally, error correction using syndromes is performed based on the all-zero syndrome to revert to an error-free state.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, because the difference between syndromes represents the channel error that has occurred, error correction based on the syndrome $s$ can correct the channel error~\cite{sd00 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Subsequently, the receiver sends an $ACK_1$ to inform the sender that $|\psi \rangle_E$ has been received.
elements.sent-2411-056: Upon receiving the $ACK_1$, the sender transmits $E_{un}$ to the receiver via a classical channel.
elements.sent-2411-057: The receiver applies $E_{un}$ to $|\psi \rangle_E$ and performs syndrome extraction again to verify if an all-zero syndrome is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-058: If the syndrome is not an all-zero, the receiver assumes a potential eavesdropping attack and aborts the process.
elements.sent-2411-059: If an all-zero syndrome is extracted, the receiver sends an $ACK_2$ to the sender again indicating the extraction of an all-zero syndrome.
elements.sent-2411-060: Upon receiving the $ACK_2$, the sender transmits a random permutation operator $P_{\kappa^4}$ and $U_{MUB}$ through the classical channel.
elements.sent-2411-061: Subsequently, the receiver measures each mutually unbiased state in the corresponding $U_{MUB}$ to verify the consistency between the results encoded by $U_{MUB}$ and the measurement outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-062: If errors are detected, it is assumed that an eavesdropper attempts to extract information, and the process is aborted.
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{de1.eps}} \caption{Distance extension of the proposed scheme Relay nodes can perform error correction based on $s$, ensuring that relay nodes cannot obtain any information about the quantum states.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The advantage of the proposed approach lies in its ability to extend distance despite encryption, as error correction is still feasible.
elements.sent-2411-064: Similar to what the receiver performs in Decryption and decoding, relay nodes perform error correction based on $s$ and then pass it to the next node, enabling reliable transmission.
elements.sent-2411-065: Furthermore, because $E_{un}$ is injected, relay nodes cannot obtain any information regarding the quantum states through syndrome extraction~\cite{qec07 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section, we analyze the security of the proposed scheme.
elements.sent-2411-067: First, we examine the countermeasures against intercept-and-resend attacks and then analyze the potential information leakage to the eavesdropper through accessible information~\cite{ai00, ai01, ai02 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Intercept-and-resend attacks involve an eavesdropper intercepting the transmitted quantum state $|\psi \rangle_E$ and sending a spoofed quantum state with a spoofed injected error $E^{' _{un}$ that matches the syndrome $s$ to the receiver, masquerading as the sender.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Subsequently, the eavesdropper intercepts the $E_{un}$ sent by the legitimate sender to extract information from $|\psi \rangle_E$.
elements.sent-2411-070: This attack can be detected because when the receiver applies the received $E_{un}$ to the spoofed quantum state of the eavesdropper, the difference between $E_{un}$ and $E^{' _{un}$ results in a non-zero syndrome during the subsequent syndrome extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this case, it could be problematic if the eavesdropper retains the information of $|\psi' \rangle$ using the intercepted quantum state and $E_{un}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: To prevent this, dummy states $|D\rangle$ defined in MUBs are mixed with $|\psi'\rangle$, and this information is only disclosed when the sender receives the $ACK_1$ indicating an all-zero syndrome.
elements.sent-2411-073: This permutation-based security relies on $P_{\kappa^4}$, that is, the number of all possible combinations of the key $\kappa^4$, denoted as $|\kappa^4|$~\cite{ps00, ps01 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: In the proposed scheme, the probability that an eavesdropper can extract $|\psi'\rangle$ is ${k\choose k' ^{-1}$.}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Accessible information available to the eavesdropper
elements.sent-2411-075: To derive accessible information, the states that an eavesdropper can perceive should be outlined with respect to the quantum state sent by the sender.
elements.sent-2411-076: As the eavesdropper does not know the data that the sender intends to transmit, it can only perceive the state as a mixture of all possible data.
elements.sent-2411-077: In addition, because of the influence of the data and randomly permuted dummy states $|D\rangle$ defined in MUBs, $E_{un}$ appears as a state in which all possible Pauli error patterns are mixed.
elements.sent-2411-078: Thus, when considering only the data, uncorrectable error applied to the state differs from the actual injected $E_{un}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: This can be mathematically verified as \begin{equation}|\psi \rangle_T = E_{un}U_E U_c |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'} \nonumber = E_{un} U^{'}_c U_E |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'}, \label{sec}\end{equation} where $U^{' _c=U_E U_c U^{-1 _E$.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}|\psi \rangle_T = E_{un}U_E U_c |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'} \nonumber = E_{un} U^{'}_c U_E |\psi' \rangle |0\rangle^{\otimes n-k'}, \label{sec}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus, although the eavesdropper can narrow down the candidate $E_{un}$ based on the syndrome information $s$, they must identify $E_{un U^{' _c$, which necessitates the consideration of all possible Pauli error patterns~\cite{ps00, ps01 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Consequently, the description of the quantum state after the receiver's error correction process perceived by the eavesdropper, $\rho_{E}$, \begin{equation}\rho_{E} = \frac{1}{2^{k'}} \frac{1}{4^n} \sum_{i=0}^{2^{k'}-1}\sum_{\mathbf{j},\mathbf{k}\in \{0,1\}^{n}} (-1)^{\mathbf{j}\wedge \mathbf{k} }X^{\mathbf{j}}Z^{\mathbf{k}}|\psi \rangle_{L,i} \langle \psi |Z^{\mathbf{k}}X^{\mathbf{j}},\end{equation} where $|\psi \rangle_{L,i}$ denotes the logical basis for $|m_i\rangle$, and $\wedge$ is a logical AND operation.
elements.sent-2411-082: The accessible information is defined as the maximum mutual information, $I_{acc (M;E)$, \begin{equation}I_{acc}(M;E) = \max_{\Lambda} I(M;Y).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-083: According to the convexity of mutual information, the maximum can be achieved through a positive operator-valued measure, $\{\Lambda_y\}$ with rank-one elements, such that $\Lambda_y \geq 0$, $\sum_{y \Lambda_y = \mathds{I}$~\cite{ai01, povm00 , \begin{equation}\Lambda_y=\mu_y |\phi_y \rangle \langle \phi_y|,\end{equation} where $|\phi_y \rangle$ are unit vectors and $\mu_y$ are positive numbers such that $\sum_{y \mu_y=2^n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The measurement results follow the probability distribution: \begin{equation}p_{Y}(y)=\mu_y \langle \phi_y|\rho_{E}|\phi_y \rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: For a given $m$, one of the entire basis of $|\psi'\rangle$, the conditional probability of a measurement outcome is \begin{equation}p_{Y|M=m}(y)=\mu_y \langle \phi_y|\rho^{m}_{E}|\phi_y \rangle.\end{equation}. with \begin{equation}\rho^{m}_{E}=\frac{1}{4^n} \sum_{\mathbf{j},\mathbf{k}\in \{0,1\}^{n}} (-1)^{\mathbf{j}\wedge \mathbf{k} }X^{\mathbf{j}}Z^{\mathbf{k}}|\psi \rangle_{L} \langle \psi |Z^{\mathbf{k}}X^{\mathbf{j}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: The accessible information is given by \begin{equation}I_{acc}(M;E) = \max_{\Lambda} \{ -\sum_{y} p_{Y}(y)\log p_{Y}(y) \nonumber + \frac{1}{2^{k'}}\sum_{y,m} p_{Y|M=m}(y)\log p_{Y|M=m}(y) \} = \max_{\Lambda} \sum_{y} \mu_y \{ -\langle \phi_y|\rho_{E}|\phi_y \rangle\log \langle \phi_y|\rho_{E}|\phi_y \rangle \nonumber + \frac{1}{2^{k'}}\sum_{m} \langle \phi_y|\rho^{m}_{E}|\phi_y \rangle\log \langle \phi_y|\rho^{m}_{E}|\phi_y \rangle \} \label{ia}\end{equation} where the term $\mu_y$ in equation~(\ref{ia ) can be canceled.}
elements.sent-2411-087: From the perspective of an eavesdropper, errors are perceived as maximally mixed.
elements.sent-2411-088: Therefore, the higher the effective code rate, $R_{\text{eff = k'/n$, the closer $\rho_E$ and $\rho^{m _E$ approach maximally mixed states (MMSs).}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: By applying the results in~\cite{ai02 with the matrix Chernoff bound and Maurer bound, \begin{equation}\langle \phi|\rho_{E}|\phi \rangle \leq (1+\epsilon)2^{-n} \label{lb} \langle \phi|\rho^{m}_{E}|\phi \rangle \geq (1-\epsilon)2^{-n} \label{ub}\end{equation} Then, by substituting equation~(\ref{lb ) and~(\ref{ub ) into equation~(\ref{ia ), the accessible information can be obtained as \begin{equation}I_{acc}(M;E) \leq 2\epsilon n,\end{equation} where $\epsilon>0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The accessible information can become sufficiently small as the lengths of the message $k'$ increase, causing $\rho_{E}$ and $\rho^{m _{E}$ to approach MMSs.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Therefore, the quantum state can be transmitted using the proposed scheme while ensuring that the information leaked to the eavesdropper remains sufficiently negligible.
elements.sent-2411-092: Considering quantum teleportation, in which quantum states are transmitted over long distances via multiple nodes, the process initially requires several rounds of entanglement swapping.
elements.sent-2411-093: Multiple rounds of entanglement purification are also necessary to maintain high fidelity at each stage.
elements.sent-2411-094: To account for the resource overhead in this process, we assumed the existence of $2^N + 1$ nodes.
elements.sent-2411-095: In addition, let $N_{EP}$ represent the number of additional qubits required for the entanglement purification process.
elements.sent-2411-096: The minimum number of qubits, $N_{EP}$, required for entanglement purification~\cite{ep00 during long-distance entanglement distribution, assuming that all entanglement swapping operations are successful, is as follows: \begin{equation}N_{EP} = \sum_{i=0}^N N_A 2^{N-i}\times2,\end{equation} where $N_A$ is the number of ancillary qubits required for entanglement purification of a single particle.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In the case of entanglement swapping, if the total number of nodes is $2^N + 1$, the number of BSMs that must succeed can be expressed as \begin{equation}O_{ES} = \sum_{i=1}^N 2^{N-i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: To increase the success probability of BSMs, $P_{ES =1-1/2^{n_{bsm }$, we can use logical BSMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The probability that all $O_{ES}$ BSMs succeed is $(1-1/2^{n_{bsm )^{O_{ES }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore, the approximate number of repetitions required for the process to succeed minimum once is $\lceil (\frac{2^{n_{bsm {2^{n_{bsm -1 )^{O_{ES \rceil$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Finally, when the total number of nodes is $2^N + 1$, the total number of qubits $N_{T}$ required for a single successful long-distance entanglement distribution is \begin{equation}N_{T} = (N_{EP}+2O_{ES}(n_{bsm}-1))\times \lceil (\frac{2^{n_{bsm}}}{2^{n_{bsm}}-1})^{O_{ES}} \rceil.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: For example, if $N_A=2$ with a physical error rate $p=0.01$, logical BSM with $n_{bsm = 2$, and the total number of nodes is $5$ including the sender and receiver, the total number of ancilla qubits required for entanglement purification is $28$ and the total number of ancilla qubits required for logical BSMs is $6$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Consequently, the success probability is $(3/4)^3$ and the number of repetitions required for a minimum of one successful attempt is approximately $3$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Thus, $102$ qubits are required for the 5-node distance entanglement distribution.
elements.sent-2411-105: The proposed scheme is determined by the logical error probability of the quantum stabilizer code, $p_L$.
elements.sent-2411-106: The $p_L$ on a depolarizing channel with a physical error rate $p$ is given as follows~\cite{le00 : \begin{equation}p_L = 1-\sum_{i=0}^t \binom{n}{i}p^i(1-p)^{n-i}. \label{le}\end{equation} To approximate the $p_L$ of the proposed scheme, we used the following quantum singleton bound~\cite{sb00 , \begin{equation}n-k \geq 2(d-1)\end{equation} We modified this bound as follows: \begin{equation}\frac{n-k}{4} \geq t. \label{msb}\end{equation} Assuming $R=\frac{1 {2}$ and substituting the relationship between $n$, $k$, and $t$ into equation~(\ref{msb ) yields the following: \begin{equation}p_L \geq 1-\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor \frac{n}{8}\rfloor}\binom{n}{i}p^i(1-p)^{n-i}. \label{mpl}\end{equation} In the case of $102$ qubits derived from the previous quantum teleportation overhead example, considering only a physical error rate of 0.01, the $p_L$ value is at least $4.0848\times10^{-11}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: This value is lower than the performance of quantum teleportation, which is $4.1215\times10^{-6}$, assuming that all rounds prior to the final purification round of long-distance entanglement distribution and the teleportation process are noiseless.
elements.sent-2411-108: According to equation~(\ref{mpl ), the proposed scheme achieves similar performance with fewer overheads compared to entanglement purification-based schemes, when using $56$ to $63$ qubits with $p_L=4.7857\times10^{-6}$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: In this study, we demonstrated a secure quantum state transmission scheme with lower overhead and higher fidelity than those of quantum teleportation over long distances.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our scheme utilized QECCs and incorporated uncorrectable error injection to achieve reliable and secure quantum state transmission over long distances.
elements.sent-2411-111: The security of the proposed scheme was based on two primary challenges.
elements.sent-2411-112: The first involved identifying actual uncorrectable errors among different errors that share the same syndrome.
elements.sent-2411-113: The second pertained to extracting the desired data from a state in which both the data intended for transmission and dummy states were randomly permuted.
elements.sent-2411-114: By reducing the overhead and increasing fidelity, our approach can improve the feasibility and reliability of long-distance quantum communication.
elements.sent-2411-115: The research can pave the way for secure and efficient quantum networks and enhance their applications in quantum communication, distributed quantum computing, and other quantum technologies.
elements.sent-2411-116: The security of the proposed scheme is primarily determined by the number of uncorrectable errors assigned to each syndrome.
elements.sent-2411-117: The number of uncorrectable errors assigned to a syndrome, $N_u$, can be estimated as follows: \begin{equation}N_u \sim \frac{4^n-\sum_{i=0}^{t}3^i \binom{n}{i}(2^{n-k}+2^2k)}{2^{n-k}} \times \frac{1}{2^{n-k}}. \label{Nu}\end{equation} where $4^n$ of equation~(\ref{Nu ) represents the number of all Pauli error patterns of length $n$, and $\sum_{i=0 ^{t 3^i \binom{n {i}$ denotes the number of all errors that the QECCs can correct.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: For simplicity, we refer to this as $N_c$.
elements.sent-2411-119: The subsequent terms multiplied by $N_c$ represents the number of errors with different weights sharing the same syndrome as correctable errors within $N_c$.
elements.sent-2411-120: Between these terms, $2^{n-k}$ represents the total number of stabilizers.
elements.figure-2411-004: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.85\linewidth]{graph2.eps}} \caption{Security analysis graph This graph illustrates the order of the average number of distinct uncorrectable errors $N_u$ with the same syndrome as equation~(\ref{Nu4}).} \label{fig:secg}
elements.sent-2411-121: When multiplied by $N_c$, it represents errors that share the same syndrome and behavior as correctable errors within $N_c$.
elements.sent-2411-122: The value $2^{2k}$ represents the total number of logical Pauli operators.
elements.sent-2411-123: When it is multiplied by $N_c$, it represents errors that share the same syndrome but have different behaviors because of logical operators, thereby being uncorrectable.
elements.sent-2411-124: In other words, the numerator represents the total number of errors associated with uncorrectable syndromes.
elements.sent-2411-125: Errors with uncorrectable syndromes, even when multiplied by stabilizers, exhibit the same behavior and syndrome.
elements.sent-2411-126: Therefore, they should be considered as single errors.
elements.sent-2411-127: To account for this, we adjusted by dividing by the total number of stabilizers, $2^{n-k}$, which served as the denominator.
elements.sent-2411-128: The final $\frac{1 {2^{n-k }$ term was used to calculate the average number of uncorrectable errors allocated to each syndrome.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Here, $2^{n-k}$ represents the total number of syndromes which are bit strings of length $n-k$.
elements.sent-2411-130: Using this information, the approximate number of completely different errors, denoted as $N_u$, that share the same syndrome as any given uncorrectable error could be determined.
elements.sent-2411-131: The quantum Hamming bound is expressed as \begin{equation}n-k \geq \log\sum_{i=0}^{t}3^i \binom{n}{i} \nonumber e^{n-k} \geq \sum_{i=0}^{t}3^i \binom{n}{i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-132: Rearranging equation~(\ref{Nu ) yields \begin{equation}N_u \geq \frac{4^n-e^{n-k}(2^{n-k}+2^2k)}{2^{n-k}} \times \frac{1}{2^{n-k}} \nonumber \geq 2^{2k}-e^{n-k}(2^{k-n}+2^{4k-2n}). \label{Nu2}\end{equation} By substituting the code rate $k/n=R$, equation~(\ref{Nu2 ) can be revised as, \begin{equation}N_u \geq 2^{2Rn} (1-(\frac{e}{4})^{(1-R)n})-(\frac{e}{2})^{(1-R)n}. \label{Nu3}\end{equation} When $n$ is sufficiently large, equation~(\ref{Nu3 ) can be approximated as follows: \begin{equation}N_u = \begin{cases} \lfloor{2^{2Rn}-(\frac{e}{2})^{(1-R)n}}\rfloor, \text{if } 2^{2Rn} > (\frac{e}{2})^{(1-R)n}, 0, \text{if } 2^{2Rn} \leq (\frac{e}{2})^{(1-R)n}. \end{cases} \label{Nu4}\end{equation} As expressed in equation~(\ref{Nu4 ), the graph of $N_u$ over the range $1\leq n\leq100$ is shown in figure~\ref{fig:secg .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: As shown in figure~\ref{fig:secg , $N_u$ has an extremely small value when $R$ is less than $0.18$.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Therefore, to ensure adequate security, QECCs with a sufficiently large $R$ should be used.
elements.sent-2411-135: As mentioned in section~\ref{pa , for the case with a total of $5$ nodes and $102$ required qubits, the number of $N_u$ when $R = 0.5$ is approximately $5.0706\times 10^{30}$.}
